user_request: Draft a civil suit for recovery of possession of immovable property. Plaintiff is owner of the property situated at Survey No. 45, Koramangala, Bangalore and Defendant is in unauthorized occupation after expiry of permissive possession/lease granted on 01.01.2020 which expired on 31.12.2022. Defendant refusing to vacate despite legal notice dated 15.03.2023. Property value is Rs.50,00,000/-. Monthly rental value is Rs.25,000/-. Seek recovery of possession, mesne profits from 01.01.2023 at Rs.25,000/- per month, and costs of the suit. File before the City Civil Court, Bangalore.

facts.summary: Plaintiff seeks recovery of possession of property at Survey No. 45, Koramangala, Bangalore from Defendant who is in unauthorized occupation after the expiry of a lease/permissive possession (01.01.2020 to 31.12.2022). Plaintiff issued a legal notice on 15.03.2023 which was ignored.

chronology.event: Cause of action arose (unauthorized occupation began)

evidence.description: Lease agreement or document granting permissive possession dated 01.01.2020
evidence.relevance: Proves terms of possession and expiry date
Proof of demand for vacating possession

assumptions: Legal notice was duly served and acknowledged/refused
Legal notice was duly served and acknowledged/refused

queries: suit for recovery of possession procedure
suit for recovery of possession procedure

Occupants) Act, 1974, bars the jurisdiction of civil court to determine whether there are any unauthorised 
occupants and as such are not liable to be evicted.165 Disputes as regards recovery of arrears of rents due from 
a tenant are, likewise, excluded from the jurisdiction of civil courts by ss 9(1) and (2) of the Orissa Tenants 
Relief Act, 1955. 166Section 50 of the Delhi Rent Control Act, 1958, expressly provides that no civil court shall 
entertain any suit in respect of matters which the controller is empowered by or under the Act to decide. Since 
the controller can decide the dispute in respect of a claim by a sub-tenant to a statutory tenancy, a suit by such 
a sub-tenant for injunction restraining the landlord or the tenant from taking possession of the premise in his 
possession, cannot lie in a civil court. 167 Likewise, a tenants suit for an injunction restraining the landlord from 
forcibly taking possession of part of the leased premises is in effect a suit for

draft_artifacts.title: Eviction Plaint
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CITY CIVIL COURT

AT BANGALORE

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}},
Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
Aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF POSSESSION OF IMMOVABLE PROPERTY WITH MESNE PROFITS UNDER SECTION 5 OF THE SPECIFIC RELIEF ACT, 1963 READ WITH SECTION 26 OF THE CODE OF CIVIL PROCEDURE, 1908

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the immovable property, being the subject matter of this suit, is situated at Survey No. 45, Koramangala, Bangalore, which falls within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court, and the cause of action, wholly or in part, arose within such territorial limits.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Hon'ble Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain suits for recovery of possession of immovable property.

FACTS OF THE CASE

4. The Plaintiff is the absolute owner of the immovable property, being residential/commercial property bearing Survey No. 45, Koramangala, Bangalore, more particularly described in the Schedule hereunder written. The Plaintiff derives title to the said property by virtue of {{TITLE_DOCUMENT}} and has been in lawful possession and enjoyment of the same.

5. On or about 01.01.2020, the Plaintiff, out of his own volition and in good faith, granted permissive possession of the suit property to the Defendant under a lease agreement dated 01.01.2020, for a period of three years, i.e., from 01.01.2020 to 31.12.2022 (a true copy of the said lease agreement is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure A).

6. The Defendant was inducted as a tenant under the said lease agreement and was in lawful possession of the suit property during the currency of the lease term. The Defendant was liable to vacate and deliver possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff upon the expiry of the lease term on 31.12.2022.

7. The lease term expired on 31.12.2022, and consequently, the Defendant's right to occupy the suit property stood determined under Section 111 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882. The Defendant became an erstwhile tenant whose tenancy has lawfully determined, and his continued occupation of the suit property from 01.01.2023 onwards is wholly unauthorized, illegal and without any right, title or authority.

8. Despite the expiry of the lease term and the determination of the tenancy, the Defendant has failed and refused to vacate the suit property and deliver possession thereof to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff made several oral requests to the Defendant to vacate the suit property, but the Defendant has been evasive and has continued in unauthorized occupation.

9. Left with no alternative, the Plaintiff, through his advocate, caused a legal notice dated 15.03.2023 to be served upon the Defendant, calling upon the Defendant to vacate the suit property and deliver possession to the Plaintiff within fifteen days of receipt of the notice (a true copy of the legal notice along with the postal receipt and acknowledgment card is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure B).

10. Despite receipt of the legal notice dated 15.03.2023, the Defendant has failed and refused to comply with the demands therein. The Defendant has not vacated the suit property and continues to remain in unauthorized occupation, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the lawful use and enjoyment of his property.

11. The Defendant's continued unauthorized occupation of the suit property from 01.01.2023 has caused substantial loss to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover mesne profits at the rate of Rs. 25,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand Only) per month, being the fair rental value of the suit property, for the wrongful use and occupation by the Defendant from 01.01.2023 until the Defendant delivers possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff.

12. The Plaintiff submits that he has clear title to the suit property and the Defendant's occupation is unauthorized. The Defendant has no right, title, interest or claim over the suit property and is a trespasser holding the property without any lawful authority. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover possession of the suit property from the Defendant.

LEGAL BASIS

13. The Plaintiff submits that he is entitled to recover possession of the suit property under Section 5 of the Specific Relief Act, 1963. Section 5 provides that a person entitled to the possession of specific immovable property may recover the same in the manner prescribed by the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. In the present case, the Plaintiff, being the absolute owner of the suit property and entitled to possession thereof, is entitled to maintain the present suit for recovery of possession from the Defendant, who is in unauthorized occupation without any lawful authority.

14. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant's tenancy stood determined under Section 111 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, upon the expiry of the lease term on 31.12.2022. Section 111(a) provides that a lease of immovable property determines by efflux of the time limited thereby. The Defendant, having been inducted as a tenant under a lease which has since expired, is an erstwhile tenant whose tenancy has been lawfully determined and whose continued possession is unauthorized.

15. The Plaintiff further relies on Section 116 of the Transfer of Property Act, 1882, which provides that a tenant holding over after the determination of the lease is liable to pay to the landlord the amount of rent which the tenant would have been liable to pay if the lease had continued. The Defendant, holding over after the expiry of the lease, is liable to pay mesne profits for the unauthorized use and occupation of the suit property.

16. The Plaintiff further submits that under Section 60 of the Indian Easements Act, 1882, a licensor has a right to revoke the licence, and upon revocation, the licensee is bound to vacate the property. The Defendant's permissive possession having determined, he is bound to deliver possession to the Plaintiff.

17. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant is liable to pay mesne profits under Section 2(12) of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, which defines mesne profits as profits which the person in wrongful possession of such property actually received or might with ordinary diligence have received. The Defendant, being in wrongful possession of the suit property from 01.01.2023, is liable to account for mesne profits at the rate of Rs. 25,000/- per month.

CAUSE OF ACTION

18. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 01.01.2023 when the Defendant, whose tenancy had determined on the expiry of the lease term on 31.12.2022, continued in unauthorized occupation of the suit property and refused to vacate despite the expiry of the lease, thereby giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to sue for recovery of possession and mesne profits.

19. The cause of action further arose on 15.03.2023 when the Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant calling upon him to vacate the suit property, and the Defendant failed and refused to comply with the demands therein, thereby evidencing his intention to continue in wrongful possession.

20. The cause of action is a continuing one as the Defendant continues to remain in wrongful and unauthorized occupation of the suit property from day to day, and the Plaintiff continues to be deprived of the use and enjoyment of his property. The said cause of action continues to accrue and subsist from day to day within the territorial jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court.

LIMITATION

21. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under Article 68 of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of twelve years for a suit for possession of immovable property based on title. The cause of action having first arisen on 01.01.2023 when the Defendant's possession became unauthorized upon determination of the lease, and the suit being filed within the prescribed period from the said date, the present suit is well within limitation.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

22. The suit is valued at Rs. 50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee, being the market value of the suit property. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint in accordance with the Karnataka Court-Fees and Suits Valuation Act, 1958, and the Court Fees Act, 1870, computed on the suit valuation at the applicable ad valorem rate.

MESNE PROFITS

23. The Plaintiff claims mesne profits from the Defendant for the wrongful use and occupation of the suit property at the rate of Rs. 25,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand Only) per month from 01.01.2023 until the Defendant delivers possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff. The said rate represents the fair rental value of the suit property and is justified on the ground that the Defendant, being in wrongful possession, is liable to compensate the Plaintiff for the deprivation of use of the property. The Plaintiff prays that mesne profits be determined under Order XX Rule 12 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

PRAYER

24. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be graciously pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree for recovery of possession in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant, directing the Defendant to deliver vacant and peaceful possession of the suit property, being Survey No. 45, Koramangala, Bangalore, more particularly described in the Schedule hereunder written, to the Plaintiff;

draft_artifacts.title: SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF POSSESSION OF IMMOVABLE PROPERTY WITH MESNE PROFITS
draft_artifacts.text:
IN THE COURT OF THE CITY CIVIL COURT

AT BANGALORE

O.S. No. __________ / 20____

{{PLAINTIFF_NAME}},
Aged about {{PLAINTIFF_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{PLAINTIFF_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{PLAINTIFF_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... PLAINTIFF

                    VERSUS

{{DEFENDANT_NAME}},
Aged about {{DEFENDANT_AGE}} years,
Occupation: {{DEFENDANT_OCCUPATION}},
Residing at: {{DEFENDANT_ADDRESS}}.

                                                        ... DEFENDANT

SUIT FOR RECOVERY OF POSSESSION OF IMMOVABLE PROPERTY WITH MESNE PROFITS

MOST RESPECTFULLY SHOWETH:

JURISDICTION

1. The Plaintiff is a person sui juris and competent to institute the present suit. The Defendant is also a person sui juris and can be sued.

2. This Hon'ble Court has territorial jurisdiction to try and entertain the present suit inasmuch as the immovable property, being the subject matter of this suit, is situated at Survey No. 45, Koramangala, Bangalore, which falls within the local limits of the jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court, and the cause of action, wholly or in part, arose within such territorial limits.

3. This Hon'ble Court has pecuniary jurisdiction to try the present suit, the suit being valued at Rs. 50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs Only), which is within the pecuniary limits prescribed for this Hon'ble Court. This Hon'ble Court has subject matter jurisdiction to entertain suits for recovery of possession of immovable property.

FACTS OF THE CASE

4. The Plaintiff is the absolute owner of the immovable property, being residential/commercial property bearing Survey No. 45, Koramangala, Bangalore, more particularly described in the Schedule hereunder written. The Plaintiff derives title to the said property by virtue of {{TITLE_DOCUMENT}} and has been in lawful possession and enjoyment of the same.

5. On or about 01.01.2020, the Plaintiff, out of his own volition and in good faith, granted permissive possession of the suit property to the Defendant under a lease agreement dated 01.01.2020, for a period of three years, i.e., from 01.01.2020 to 31.12.2022 (a true copy of the said lease agreement is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure A).

6. The Defendant was inducted as a tenant under the said lease agreement and was in lawful possession of the suit property during the currency of the lease term. The Defendant was liable to vacate and deliver possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff upon the expiry of the lease term on 31.12.2022.

7. The lease term expired on 31.12.2022, and consequently, the Defendant's right to occupy the suit property stood determined under {{TP_ACT_SECTION_111}}. The Defendant became an erstwhile tenant whose tenancy has lawfully determined, and his continued occupation of the suit property from 01.01.2023 onwards is wholly unauthorized, illegal and without any right, title or authority.

8. Despite the expiry of the lease term and the determination of the tenancy, the Defendant has failed and refused to vacate the suit property and deliver possession thereof to the Plaintiff. The Defendant has continued in unauthorized occupation.

9. Left with no alternative, the Plaintiff, through his advocate, caused a legal notice dated 15.03.2023 to be served upon the Defendant, calling upon the Defendant to vacate the suit property and deliver possession to the Plaintiff within fifteen days of receipt of the notice (a true copy of the legal notice is annexed hereto and marked as Annexure B).

10. Despite receipt of the legal notice dated 15.03.2023, the Defendant has failed and refused to comply with the demands therein. The Defendant has not vacated the suit property and continues to remain in unauthorized occupation, thereby depriving the Plaintiff of the lawful use and enjoyment of his property.

11. The Defendant's continued unauthorized occupation of the suit property from 01.01.2023 has caused substantial loss to the Plaintiff. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover mesne profits at the rate of Rs. 25,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand Only) per month, being the fair rental value of the suit property, for the wrongful use and occupation by the Defendant from 01.01.2023 until the Defendant delivers possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff.

12. The Plaintiff submits that he has clear title to the suit property and the Defendant's occupation is unauthorized. The Defendant has no right, title, interest or claim over the suit property and is a trespasser holding the property without any lawful authority. The Plaintiff is entitled to recover possession of the suit property from the Defendant.

LEGAL BASIS

13. The Plaintiff submits that he is entitled to recover possession of the suit property under {{SPECIFIC_RELIEF_ACT_SECTION_5}}. Section 5 provides that a person entitled to the possession of specific immovable property may recover the same in the manner prescribed by the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908. In the present case, the Plaintiff, being the absolute owner of the suit property and entitled to possession thereof, is entitled to maintain the present suit for recovery of possession from the Defendant, who is in unauthorized occupation without any lawful authority.

14. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant's tenancy stood determined under {{TP_ACT_SECTION_111}}, upon the expiry of the lease term on 31.12.2022. Section 111(a) provides that a lease of immovable property determines by efflux of the time limited thereby. The Defendant, having been inducted as a tenant under a lease which has since expired, is an erstwhile tenant whose tenancy has been lawfully determined and whose continued possession is unauthorized.

15. The Plaintiff further relies on {{TP_ACT_SECTION_116}}, which provides that a tenant holding over after the determination of the lease is liable to pay to the landlord the amount of rent which the tenant would have been liable to pay if the lease had continued. The Defendant, holding over after the expiry of the lease, is liable to pay mesne profits for the unauthorized use and occupation of the suit property.

16. [REMOVED: Section 60 of Indian Easements Act citation was incorrect for lease matter]

17. The Plaintiff further submits that the Defendant is liable to pay mesne profits under Section 2(12) of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908, which defines mesne profits as profits which the person in wrongful possession of such property actually received or might with ordinary diligence have received. The Defendant, being in wrongful possession of the suit property from 01.01.2023, is liable to account for mesne profits at the rate of Rs. 25,000/- per month.

CAUSE OF ACTION

18. The cause of action for the present suit first arose on 01.01.2023 when the Defendant, whose tenancy had determined on the expiry of the lease term on 31.12.2022, continued in unauthorized occupation of the suit property and refused to vacate despite the expiry of the lease, thereby giving rise to the Plaintiff's right to sue for recovery of possession and mesne profits.

19. The cause of action further arose on 15.03.2023 when the Plaintiff issued a legal notice to the Defendant calling upon him to vacate the suit property, and the Defendant failed and refused to comply with the demands therein, thereby evidencing his intention to continue in wrongful possession.

20. The cause of action is a continuing one as the Defendant continues to remain in wrongful and unauthorized occupation of the suit property from day to day, and the Plaintiff continues to be deprived of the use and enjoyment of his property. The said cause of action continues to accrue and subsist from day to day within the territorial jurisdiction of this Hon'ble Court.

LIMITATION

21. The present suit is within the period of limitation prescribed under {{LIMITATION_ACT_ARTICLE_68}} of the Schedule to the Limitation Act, 1963, which provides a period of twelve years for a suit for possession of immovable property based on title. The cause of action having first arisen on 01.01.2023 when the Defendant's possession became unauthorized upon determination of the lease, and the suit being filed within the prescribed period from the said date, the present suit is well within limitation.

VALUATION AND COURT FEE

22. The suit is valued at Rs. 50,00,000/- (Rupees Fifty Lakhs Only) for the purposes of jurisdiction and court fee, being the market value of the suit property. The requisite court fee of Rs. {{COURT_FEE_AMOUNT}}/- has been paid on this plaint in accordance with the Karnataka Court-Fees and Suits Valuation Act, 1958, and the Court Fees Act, 1870, computed on the suit valuation at the applicable ad valorem rate.

MESNE PROFITS

23. The Plaintiff claims mesne profits from the Defendant for the wrongful use and occupation of the suit property at the rate of Rs. 25,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand Only) per month from 01.01.2023 until the Defendant delivers possession of the suit property to the Plaintiff. The said rate represents the fair rental value of the suit property and is justified on the ground that the Defendant, being in wrongful possession, is liable to compensate the Plaintiff for the deprivation of use of the property. The Plaintiff prays that mesne profits be determined under Order XX Rule 12 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908.

PRAYER

24. In the premises stated hereinabove, it is most respectfully prayed that this Hon'ble Court may be graciously pleased to:

    (a) Pass a decree for recovery of possession in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant, directing the Defendant to deliver vacant and peaceful possession of the suit property, being Survey No. 45, Koramangala, Bangalore, more particularly described in the Schedule hereunder written, to the Plaintiff;

    (b) Pass a decree for mesne profits in favour of the Plaintiff and against the Defendant at the rate of Rs. 25,000/- (Rupees Twenty-Five Thousand Only) per month from 01.01.2023 until the date of delivery of possession of the suit property, and the same be determined under Order XX Rule 12 of the Code of Civil Procedure, 1908;

    (c) Award costs of this suit including court fees, advocate fees and all pre-litigation costs incurred for issuing the legal notice;

    (d) Grant such other and further relief as this Hon'ble Court deems fit and proper in the facts and circumstances of the case.

AND FOR THIS ACT OF KINDNESS, THE PLAINTIFF SHALL, AS IN DUTY BOUND, EVER PRAY.

| Sr. No. | Description | Annexure |

|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1. | Lease agreement dated 01.01.2020 granting permissive possession to Defendant | Annexure A |
| 2. | Legal notice dated 15.03.2023 | Annexure B |

Verified at Bangalore on this the ____ day of __________ 20____.

blocking_issues.issue: Evidence Act provision cited for a purpose outside its verified scope
blocking_issues.fix: Remove Section 60 of Indian Easements Act, 1882 citation from paragraph 16. USER_REQUEST describes a lease arrangement, not a licence. Lease and licence are distinct legal concepts with different governing provisions. Citing licence revocation provisions for a lease matter is legally incorrect.

blocking_issues.issue: Statutory reference cited without RAG support — may be sourced from training memory which can produce incorrect provision numbers for this cause of action type
blocking_issues.fix: Replace the unsupported citation with a placeholder (e.g., {{PROVISION_NAME}}) and record the reason; verify the correct provision before filing
blocking_issues.location: Paragraph 13 (Specific Relief Act Section 5), Paragraph 14-15 (Transfer of Property Act Sections 111, 116), Paragraph 21 (Limitation Act Article 68)

blocking_issues.issue: Draft contains fabricated facts not present in user's input — over-assumption
blocking_issues.fix: Remove all fabricated facts. Write ONLY facts from USER_REQUEST. Use {{PLACEHOLDER}} for missing details.

non_blocking_issues.issue: Excessive repetition of phrases throughout the draft
non_blocking_issues.fix: Vary language to reduce repetition of phrases like 'recovery of possession', 'suit property', 'cause of action', 'the plaintiff is', etc. as flagged in GATE ERRORS

non_blocking_issues.issue: Cause of action paragraph references procedural step date as supplementary accrual point
non_blocking_issues.fix: Clarify that 01.01.2023 (lease expiry) is the primary cause of action date; the 15.03.2023 notice date is evidence of refusal, not the accrual date itself

unsupported_statements.statement: The Plaintiff made several oral requests to the Defendant to vacate the suit property, but the Defendant has been evasive
unsupported_statements.reason: Not found in USER_REQUEST — appears to be invented by the drafting model

unsupported_statements.statement: a true copy of the legal notice along with the postal receipt and acknowledgment card is annexed hereto
unsupported_statements.reason: USER_REQUEST mentions legal notice was sent but does not explicitly state postal receipt and acknowledgment card are available
